A new wrinkle

Dianna Hunt Fort Worth Star-Telegram July/August 2001
Investigative MethodsEducation

A Fort Worth high school football coach resigned after sexual assault allegations in a deal involving a $100,000 payout, prompting investigation into Texas's teacher licensing system which was found to have a massive backlog allowing credentialed teachers with serious criminal histories to remain teaching for years.

It started with a high-profile football coach and allegations of sexual assault. The story made headlines in the Fort Worth area as a local school district investigated the allegations against a popular high school coach. The coach eventually resigned from the district after officials agreed to pay him $100,000 and drop the investigation. He was never charged with assaulting a female coach but was charged later with harassing another co-worker and her boyfriend.

Star-Telegram education reporter Mart Frazier called the state teacher licensing board which acknowledged it was investigating the coach's teaching credentials. Further investigation found that convicted child molesters, rapists, drug users, and thieves, in addition to the coach, had remained certified as Texas teachers, sometimes for years, while their credentials were under investigation. State and school officials knew the teachers had problems but could not act quickly enough due to an overwhelmed system with a backlog of cases that could take years to resolve.
